coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A over N,S-doped carbon quantum dot-modified MIL-101(Fe) heterostructure composites under visible light irradiation by persulfate
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, He
Zhong, Yan
Tian, Kaixun
Pang, Haoliang
Hao, Yaoyao
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen- and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot-modified MIL-101(Fe) (N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)) heterojunction for visible light-induced activation of persulfate (PS) for bisphenol A (BPA) degradation was studied. The photocatalytic performance of BPA degradation was remarkably promoted by the composite material under the simultaneous action of PS and visible light irradiation. BPA photodegradation (100%) was achieved over 60 min with 0.4 g/L of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composite and 3 mM PS at natural pH. The degradation kinetics rate constants (k) approached 3.6 times higher than that of MIL-101(Fe). The enhanced performance in BPA degradation can be ascribed to the interfacial transference of photoinduced electrons from N,S:CQD to MIL-101(Fe), leading to the valid separation of electron-hole pairs and greatly accelerating PS activation to generate highly reactive SO4•−. Both holes (h+), sulfate radicals (SO4•−), superoxide radicals (•O2 −), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) contributed to BPA degradation according to radical quenching experiments. N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) also exhibited great reusability and excellent catalytic performance after four consecutive cycles of use. This work provides valid organic contaminant degradation under the dual action of visible light and PS with a novel perspective on the fabrication of heterojunction photocatalysts ground on Fe-based MOFs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-101(Fe)
N,S:CQD
Persulfate
Heterojunction
Photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation of the Hunan Provincial Education Department
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of the Hunan Provincial Education Department (20A470).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites; (b) FT-IR spectra of MIL-101(Fe), N,S:CQD and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites.

SEM image of (a) MIL-101(Fe) and (b) N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe); (c) TEM image and the corresponding size distribution of N,S:CQD; (d) TEM* image of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe); (e-j) Dark-field STEM images and corresponding elemental HAADF-STEM mapping image of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe); (k) Scheme diagram of the synthesis procedure of the composite material.
XPS spectra of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe): (a) survey; (b) C1 s; (c) O1 s; (d) Fe2p; (e) N1 s; (f) S2p.
(a) UV–vis diffusion reflection spectra and (b) plot of (αhν)2 vs. photon energy (hν) and (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the as-synthesized samples.

(a) The adsorption of BPA over MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites in the dark; (b) Photocatalytic activities of BPA over N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) with different contents of N,S-CQD under visible light irradiation; (c) Photocatalytic activities of BPA degradation over MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites under visible light irradiation; (d) Comparison of kinetic constants of BPA degradation with different systems; (e) the calculated corresponding k of different systems.
The degradation of BPA in deionized water, tap water and river water.

(a) Degradation of BPA over the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composite at multiple regeneration cycles; (b) The concentration of iron leaching from the catalysts to the aqueous phase at pH 7.0; (c) The degradation of BPA with Fe3+/PS/Vis and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis. The concentration of Fe3+ was selected to be 1.46 mg/L through the concentration identification of iron leaching of the catalyst (N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis).
Mott-Schottky plot of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe).
(a) PL spectra and (b) time-resolved PL spectra of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe); (c) transient photocurrent responses and (d) EIS Nyquist plots of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe).
(a) Reactive species trapping experiments of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) with PS under visible light irradiation; (b) the corresponding reaction constants.
Possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A over N,S-doped carbon quantum dot-modified MIL-101(Fe) heterostructure composites under visible light irradiation by persulfate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen- and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot-modified MIL-101(Fe) (N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)) heterojunction for visible light-induced activation of persulfate (PS) for bisphenol A (BPA) degradation was studied. The photocatalytic performance of BPA degradation was remarkably promoted by the composite material under the simultaneous action of PS and visible light irradiation. BPA photodegradation (100%) was achieved over 60 min with 0.4 g/L of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composite and 3 mM PS at natural pH. The degradation kinetics rate constants (k) approached 3.6 times higher than that of MIL-101(Fe). The enhanced performance in BPA degradation can be ascribed to the interfacial transference of photoinduced electrons from N,S:CQD to MIL-101(Fe), leading to the valid separation of electron-hole pairs and greatly accelerating PS activation to generate highly reactive SO4•−. Both holes (h+), sulfate radicals (SO4•−), superoxide radicals (•O2 −), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) contributed to BPA degradation according to radical quenching experiments. N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) also exhibited great reusability and excellent catalytic performance after four consecutive cycles of use. This work provides valid organic contaminant degradation under the dual action of visible light and PS with a novel perspective on the fabrication of heterojunction photocatalysts ground on Fe-based MOFs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, ever-increasing water environmental pollution, which is caused by uncontrolled industrial wastewater discharge, has attracted extensive attention. Among all kinds of pollutants, endocrine-disrupting compounds are considered to be emerging contaminants that have persistence and obvious estrogen-like activity and can be latent endangers to ecological and human bodies [1]. As a typical endocrine-disrupting compound, bisphenol A (BPA) is often used in the manufacture of polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins [2]. BPA leaked from packaging and storage containers is widely present in water and food [3,4]. Many toxicity tests have shown that BPA exhibits developmental and reproductive toxicities against many kinds of animals. Therefore, the development of new technologies to eliminate BPA from wastewater is generally regarded as an indispensable task for a sustainable society.

Among all water treatment technologies, the sulfate radical (SO4•−)-based advanced oxidation process (SR-AOP) is a valid technique for recalcitrant contamination treatment by creating highly reactive SO4•− [5]. Compared with hydroxyl radicals (•OH), SO4•− has some intrinsic advantages, such as a higher redox potential (2.5–3.1 V), wider pH adaptation range (3 < pH < 9), and longer half-life time (4 × 10-5 s) [6]. SO4•− is produced via persulfate activation. There are many methods to activate PS, including transition metal ions (Ag+, Fe2+, etc.), heat, ultrasonic waves, and heterogeneous photocatalysis [7,8]. Among the various activation approaches, researchers have paid increasing attention to photocatalytically induced persulfate activation due to its use of clean energy and mild reaction conditions [9].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are composed of a porous three-dimensional crystal structure between metal-containing nodes and organic bridging linkers, have generated rapid development in several fields, such as selective adsorption and separation, light harvesting, sensors and catalysis, due to their adjustable structural composition, high surface areas, and well-defined porous structures [10]. Fe-based MOFs are feasible for photocatalytic processes because of the richness of iron-oxo clusters and abundant active sites [11]. Abundant trivalent iron ion (Fe(III)) metal nodes are included in these MOFs. Fe(III) is reduced to Fe(II) because of photogenerated electron transfer between the metal node and the organic linker, which can efficiently induce oxidants to produce active species [12]. Recently, a few important studies have demonstrated that organic pollutants can be degraded by Fe-based MOFs under the dual effects of visible light and PS. For instance, Gao et al. reported that MIL-53(Fe) exhibits powerful excitation of PS for efficient photocatalytic reaction in organic dyes under visible light irradiation [13]. Hu et al. directly applied MIL-101(Fe) to activate PS for organophosphorus flame retardant degradation under different irradiation wavelengths [14]. However, some weaknesses of Fe-based MOF photocatalysts still exist, such as reusability and the limitation of photocatalytic efficiencies due to the quick recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes [15]. Therefore, further development of new approaches to modify Fe-based MOFs to enhance their performance for PS activation under visible light is worthy of further exploration.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is novel quasi-zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have already received extensive attention because of their distinct upconversion photoluminescence and excellent electron-donating capability [16,17]. According to relevant reports, the introduction of CQD onto a photocatalyst can improve the charge transfer rate, decrease charge recombination, and harvest light effectively [18]. Recent research has established that supporting CQD on MOFs can greatly improve photocatalytic efficiency and help regenerate the material [19,20]. Moreover, heteroatom (such as nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus) doping can efficiently encourage charge delocalization and decrease the work function of carbon. The electron transfer capability of CQD can be efficiently promoted [21]. Based on the above studies, we envision that heteroatom-doped CQD will accelerate the photogenerated electron transport of Fe-based MOFs, which may be a highly efficient activation of PS for the photodegradation of recalcitrant pollutants. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, few people have conducted in-depth research on such methods.

In this paper, we first synthesized nitrogen- and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S:CQD) by a hydrothermal method. Then, N,S:CQD-modified MIL-101(Fe) (N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)) was further obtained after mixing with the ligand metal precursor. Meanwhile, BPA was chosen as a target pollutant. The light could be collected more effectively, and the interfacial charge was efficiently separated by this composite. At the same time, PS activation was also provided with plentiful active sites. As a result, N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) demonstrates high efficiencies for the degradation of BPA under the action of visible light and PS due to its high photocatalytic activity. We also proposed the corresponding reaction mechanism based on the experimental results, and we found that N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) still has high reactivity after four recycling experiments.
All chemicals are provided in Text S1 of the Supporting Information.

MIL-101(Fe) was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method on the basis of former reports with slight adjustment [22]. In a typical procedure, 1.1674 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.3562 g H2BDC were put into 70 mL DMF solution. The solution was churned for 30 min in total at room temperature, then put into a 100 mL Teflon liner and heated to 110 °C for 20 h. The acquired products were put into an ethanol solution and churned at 60 °C for 30 min to completely remove the residual solvent. The light-yellow powder was collected by centrifugation and drying at 60 °C in a vacuum oven overnight.

N,S:CQD was prepared by a hydrothermal method on the basis of former reports with slight adjustment [23]. Typically, 1 mM thiourea and 1 mM sodium citrate were evenly ground in a mortar for 5 min to be well mixed. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave filled with deionized water, which contained 80% of the autoclave capacity (100 mL) by volume, and heated to 180 °C in a drying oven for 5 h. The acquired solution was filtered to filter out the larger particle impurities using a syringe filter (pore diameter, 0.22 m) after cooling naturally. The as-obtained product was dialyzed in pure water with a dialysis membrane for 12 h to obtain an N,S:CQD aqueous solution. To acquire the N,S:CQD solid, the N,S:CQD solution was freeze-dried for 24 h afterward.

A quantitatively prepared N,S:CQD sample was premixed with the ligand solution. After that, the metal precursor was evenly mixed with the N,S:CQD-ligand solution. The remaining procedure referred to the subsequent synthesis steps of MIL-101(Fe). Referring to the above procedure, a range of composites, which were modified with different N,S:CQD contents (1%, 3%, 5%), were also prepared and denoted as 1-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) and 5-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on a D/Max 2500 X-ray polycrystalline diffractometer. Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out on a Braker Alpha spectrometer. Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were taken with a Hitachi FESEM-4800. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed on a JEM-2100F microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) surveys were acquired by an ESCALAB 250Xi system (pass energy 50.0 eV) using C1 s (284.8 eV) as a reference to calibrate the binding energies. After the sample was pressed, purged, and cut, it was fixed on the sample stage with insulating double-sided tape. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the samples were collected on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2500, Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra were obtained on an FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas were recorded on a Quantachrome NOVA 2200e nitrogen adsorption apparatus.Information.

The photocatalytic ability of the sample was assessed by investigating BPA degradation with an initial concentration of 20 mg/L under visible light irradiation. A 350 W Xenon lamp with an optical filter (λ > 400 nm) was employed as a solar light source. Before irradiation and the addition of PS, the sample was dispersed into 100 mL of BPA aqueous solution with magnetic stirring for 1 h in the dark to reach absorption–desorption equilibrium. After desired intervals, 2 mL of samples were withdrawn at regular intervals (10 min) with the addition of methanol to terminate the reaction and then filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filters for high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis. The degradation percent (%) was calculated in terms of the following equation:
Degradation
percent
( % )
=
( 1 - C /
C 0
)
× 100 %
where C is the concentration of BPA at a certain reaction time (t) and C0 represents the initial concentration of BPA before illumination.
All the methods of photoelectrochemical evaluation are provided in Text S2 of the Supporting Information.

The XRD patterns for the N,S:CQD, MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites, which are modified with different weight contents of N,S:CQD, are displayed in Fig. 1 a. The wide peak centered at 2θ = 26° can be attributed to graphitic carbon [24]. The XRD patterns of MIL-101(Fe) matched those reported previously, indicating that those materials were prepared successfully [25]. When N,S:CQD was modified on the MIL-101(Fe) materials, no characteristic diffraction peak of N,S:CQD could be detected. This may be attributed to the low crystallinity and very low content of N,S:CQD in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites. Moreover, we noticed that the intensities of the characteristic diffraction peaks for the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites gradually decreased as the amount of N,S:CQD increased, in contrast with those for pure MIL-101(Fe). This phenomenon may result from the reduced periodicity of crystal lattices in the MOF after modification with N,S:CQD.

The FT-IR spectra of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites are displayed in Fig. 1b. For N,S:CQD, two characteristic peaks located at 1293 cm−1 and 1065 cm−1 can be presumed to be the typical stretching vibration of C-N and sulfur-based functional groups (C = S). For MIL-101(Fe), the large broad absorption band, which took 3418 cm−1 as a center, can be presumed to be the free solvent molecules captured by the catalyst pores. The strong bands at 1601 cm−1 and 1391 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrational stretching modes of C = O groups. The absorption bands at 1013 cm−1 and 747 cm−1 can be ascribed to the characteristic absorption peaks of the benzene ring. The bands at 551 cm−1 are considered to be Fe-O formed by the coordination of trivalent iron ions with carboxyl groups. The N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) compounds show adsorption bands analogous to those of unadulterated MIL-101(Fe). Furthermore, the two characteristic peaks located at 1293 cm−1 and 1065 cm−1 can still be found in all FT-IR spectra of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), which suggests the existence of N,S:CQD in the composites [26].

The morphological properties of the MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites were characterized by SEM and TEM techniques (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, MIL-101(Fe) manifested typical octahedral structured and smooth surfaces. The surface of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) particles becomes rougher after the addition of N,S:CQD (Fig. 2b). We noticed that MIL-101(Fe) still retained its octahedral shape after the introduction of N,S:CQD. Therefore, we concluded that the introduction of N,S:CQD will not damage the structure of MIL-101(Fe). The TEM image of N,S:CQD are presented in Fig. 2c. The N,S:CQD with a nearly spherical shape was uniformly distributed. Based on TEM analysis, N,S:CQD displayed a particle size distribution of 0.6–1.1 nm. As Fig. 2d displays, the TEM images of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) demonstrated an octahedral shape in an indirect way, and the above phenomenon was consistent with former reports. However, the N,S:CQD particles were too small to be found in the image. Therefore, the HAADF-STEM image and the relevant EDX elemental mapping were recorded and are displayed in Fig. 2f-j. This result implies that C (green), O (yellow), Fe (red), N (blue), and S (violet) elements are distributed evenly in the composite. N and S elements were detected in the compound, which provided the addition of N,S:CQD into MIL-101(Fe) with effective support. We are convinced that N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) was successfully synthesized.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were accomplished to research the elemental composition and bonding characteristics of the as-prepared products. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the survey scanning spectrum for N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) demonstrated that the composite is mainly made upof C, O, Fe, N, and S atoms. As depicted in Fig. 3b, there are four distinct peaks at 284.8, 285.3, 286.0, 288.7 eV in the C1 s spectrum. The peaks at 285.3 eV and 286 eV can be assigned to C-N and C = S present in the N,S:CQD, respectively [27], while the peaks at 284.8 and 288.7 eV can be attributed to benzoic rings and C = O of the H2BDC ligand in MIL-101(Fe) [28]. The O1 s peak of the composite can be deconvoluted into three peaks, as shown in Fig. 3c. The peak at 532.7 eV is presumed to be the water species, which was consistent with previous studies [29,30,31]. The peaks at 531.8 and 531.0 eV are presumed to be the surface hydroxyl groups and crystal lattice oxygen in the structure of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), respectively. Meanwhile, Fig. 3d shows Fe2p spectra. The binding energy values of Fe2p1/2 and Fe2p3/2 were assigned at 711.4 and 725.6 eV, respectively [32], and the two peaks at 714 and 717.6 eV confirm the existence of Fe(III) in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) structure [33]. Moreover, the N1 s spectrum shows three pronounced peaks at 399.4, 400.6, and 401.4 eV, which are presumed to be the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N or N–H bands, respectively (Fig. 3e) [34]. The high-resolution S2p spectra further prove the existence of S (Fig. 3f). Based on the above results, we believe that we have successfully introduced N,S:CQD into MIL-101(Fe).

UV–vis diffusion reflection spectra were obtained to evaluate the optical response of the samples. As Fig. 4 a displays, the absorption band of MIL-101(Fe) in the ultraviolet region was attributed to π-π* ligand transition, and the absorption band in the visible region was attributed to the transition from ligand to metal charge transfer (LMCT) from H2BDC ligand to Fe3+ center [35]. For the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) nanocomposite, the light-harvesting ability in the visible light region increased as the content of N,S:CQD increased. The absorption edge of pure MIL-101(Fe) is located at 465 nm, which is significantly shorter than that of 5-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) (537 nm). The cause of the increase in photocatalytic activity was presumably due to the increase in electron-hole pairs accompanying the increase in light absorption. Moreover, the bandgap energies (Eg) were calculated derived from Tauc plots ((αhv)2 = A(hv-Eg)). The calculated bandgaps derived from the Tauc plot (Fig. 4b) of the MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites were approximately 2.77 eV and 2.61 eV, respectively, which also demonstrated that the addition of N,S:CQD can reduce the bandgap width of MIL-101(Fe).

In addition, N2-adsorption–desorption isotherms of prepared samples were acquired to determine the specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 4c, MIL-101(Fe) has a large specific surface area that can provide a large number of active sites [36]. The specific surface areas of MIL-101(Fe) and 5-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) are calculated to be 510.9 m2/g and 346.7 m2/g, respectively. Due to the addition of N,S:CQD, the BET surface area of MIL-101(Fe) was remarkably reduced. This may be because the introduction of N,S:CQD sacrificed part of the specific surface area of MIL-101(Fe).

The photocatalytic performances of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites for BPA degradation were evaluated under the dual action of visible light irradiation and PS. Before the reaction, adsorption experiments were performed under dark conditions. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, all N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites manifested higher adsorption capability for BPA, which can be ascribed to the special nanoporous structure of MIL-101 (Fe). Under the dual action of visible light irradiation and PS, BPA degradation follows the sequence of 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) > 5-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) > 1-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) > MIL-101(Fe) (Fig. 5b). The BPA removal efficiency reached 59.6% of BPA within 60 min on the parent MIL-101(Fe). In contrast, all N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites exhibited higher BPA degradation than pure MIL-101(Fe), suggesting a synergistic effect between MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD. The much higher BPA degradation could be ascribed to the effective electron transfer from N,S:CQD to MIL-101(Fe) and the improvement of light-harvesting ability, which effectually fosters PS activation to produce more SO4•−.

The catalytic capabilities of the MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) systems were contrasted in the degradation process of BPA to analyze the reaction mechanism in depth. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, no decomposition of BPA was discovered under visible light irradiation, suggesting that BPA is photostable in water without PS and catalyst. The N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) systems manifested higher photocatalytic activities than their MIL-101(Fe) counterparts, which may be ascribed to the synergistic effect of N,S:CQD and MIL-101(Fe). Moreover, we found that the degradation efficiencies for BPA in the MIL-101(Fe)/PS and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS systems were 23.2% and 39.9%, respectively, which may be due to the excellent activity of MIL-101(Fe) for PS activation. In particular, the removal efficiency of BPA is increased up to 100% in the 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis systems by adding 3 mM PS. The improvement in BPA degradation efficiency was ascribed to the synergistic effect between N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) and PS under visible light.

To further evaluate the synergistic effect of MIL-101(Fe), 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) and PS under visible light irradiation, the kinetic curves for BPA degradation were well matched to the pseudofirst-order model (Fig. 5d). Fig. 5e displays the rate constant (k) of every different reaction. The k for BPA degradation over the 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system is 0.0551 min−1, which is almost 3.6 times that of the MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system (0.01528 min−1). These results demonstrate that the incorporation of N,S:CQD can obviously enhance the synergistic effect of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system.

The influences of PS concentration and initial pH on the removal of BPA in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system were explored. As shown in Fig. S1, the degradation efficiency of BPA was enhanced as the PS dose was varied from 0 to 3 mM. Nevertheless, we did not find notable extra improvement for BPA degradation as the PS dosage reached 4 mM. This phenomenon may result from the scavenging effect of the produced SO4•− and the excess PS through equations (1,2). In addition, we investigated the impacts of initial pH on BPA degradation and displayed the impacts in Fig. S2. It was observed that the photocatalyst can work in a relatively wide initial pH range. Obviously, the removal efficiency of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis toward BPA in acidic or neutral environments is remarkably better than that in alkaline environments. This phenomenon can be ascribed to two causes: i) The catalyst carries more positive charges under acidic conditions, which can attract more S2O8 2− onto the catalyst, leading to faster reactant transport and shorter induction time [37]. ii) Under strong acidic conditions, the degradation of BPA is promoted by a homogeneous Fenton-like process, which is formed by the leaching of Fe ions from the catalyst into the water phase [38].
(1) SO4•− + S2O8 2− → S2O8•− + SO4 2−
(2) SO4•− + SO4•− → S2O8 2−

To test the performance of the catalyst in practical applications, deionized water (DW), tap water (TW) and water from the Xiangjiang River (RW) were selected as the water matrix to carry out the BPA degradation experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 , the degradation rate of BPA in deionized water is 100%, while the degradation rates in tap water and river water drop to 90.1% and 81.7%, respectively. The decrease in degradation efficiency of BPA may be related to the presence of anions and organics in the water. To achieve a better removal effect, the amount of catalyst can be appropriately increased according to actual needs.

The durability of a catalyst is an essential property in actual applications. We detached the catalyst from the aqueous suspension to assess its recyclability after each experiment. The catalyst material obtained by separation was cleaned and dried for 8 h at 60 °C before the next experiment. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after the catalyst was recycled four times, the removal efficiency of BPA was still as high as 91.7%, indicating that the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composite exhibited excellent catalytic stability. The leaching amount of iron ions from the catalyst was also measured. As shown in Fig. 7b, the iron leaching rate of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) was much lower than that of MIL-101(Fe), illustrating that the addition of N,S:CQD effectively promotes the stability of the catalyst [39]. Subsequently, the effect of iron leaching of the catalyst was determined. As shown in Fig. 7c, the removal rate of BPA by Fe3+/PS/Vis is only 4.9%, which is close to the removal rate under the same conditions without Fe3+ reaction. This result indicated that the iron leaching of the catalyst contributes little to the reaction.

We conducted a Mott-Schottky test to explore the cause of the enhanced catalytic activities of N,S:CQD on composite materials. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the measured sample curves show a positive slope, indicating that the samples have n-type semiconductor characteristics [40]. Substituting the test data into the Mott-Schottky formula, using the Ag/AgCl reference electrode as a control, the flat band potentials (Efb) of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) are 0.195 V and 0.240 V, respectively. At the same time, we know that the potential of the standard hydrogen electrode (0.197 V) is higher than that of the standard silver chloride electrode [41]. For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band potential (Ecb) is approximately 0.1 V smaller than the Efb [42]. Therefore, the Ecb of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) are roughly calculated to be approximately 0.537 V and 0.492 V when compared with a standard hydrogen electrode. The valence band potentials (Evb) of MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) are calculated to be 3.307 V and 3.102 V, respectively, which are derived from the equation Evb = Ecb + Eg. After introducing N,S:CQD, the Ecb of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) has a negative shift of 0.045 V, which can effectively strengthen the thermodynamic driving force of photocatalytic proton reduction, thereby promoting persulfate activation.

To check the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers of the as-synthesized samples, photoluminescence spectroscopy was employed. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) nanocomposite manifests the lowest PL intensity in contrast to MIL-101(Fe), illustrating that the separation of electron-hole pairs was significantly promoted [43]. The Mott-Schottky effect significantly reduces the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which proves the formation of a Mott Schottky heterojunction between N,S:CQD and MIL-101(Fe) [44]. In addition, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra were applied to research the charge transfer in the as-synthesized samples. As displayed in Fig. 9b, the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers in N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) was significantly prolonged compared with MIL-101(Fe). The recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be restrained by the addition of N,S:CQD [45]. Moreover, we could also obtain the same consequence from the steady-state PL analysis.

To clearly evaluate the role of N,S:CQD in N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), the photoelectrochemical analysis of the samples was also carried out. Compared with N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), the single MIL-101(Fe) manifests a lower photocurrent density (Fig. 9c), which may be ascribedto the faster combination of holes and photogenerated electrons in MIL-101(Fe) [46]. The enhanced photocurrent density of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) was ascribed to the heterostructure between MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD, which increased the transfer and separation efficiency of photogenerated charges [47]. In the comparison of the diameter of the Nyquist semicircle, the diameter of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composite material is manifestly smaller than that of MIL-101(Fe) (Fig. 9d). We can conclude that the resistance of the composite is lower than the resistance of MIL-101(Fe). As a result, the effective transport of photoinduced electrons between N,S:CQD and MIL-101(Fe) promotes electron-hole separation, and this conclusion is consistent with its better photocatalytic reactivity for BPA degradation.

To clearly explain the contribution of various kinds of reactive species in the catalytic process of the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system, active species trapping experiments were carried out utilizing AO (scavenger of h+), benzoquinone (BQ, scavenger of •O2 −), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, scavenger of •OH) [48]. The effect of the addition of different scavengers on the degradation efficiency of BPA is displayed in Fig. 10 a and b, and the inhibition efficiency of BPA degradation with different trapping reagents is as follows: AO > BQ > MeOH > IPA. The kinetic constants for BPA degradation increased from 0.00525 to 0.02379 min−1, suggesting that all the scavengers can inhibit the degradation of BPA. Notably, we found that the degradation efficiency of BPA was obviously reduced when AO and BQ existed in the system, which confirmed that h+ and •O2 − were the principal oxidizing species in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system for BPA degradation. Meanwhile, the degradation efficiency of BPA also decreased when MeOH and IPA existed, indicating that •OH and SO4•− take part in the oxidation of BPA.

From the abovementioned conclusion, a realistic degradation mechanism of BPA in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system is proposed (Fig. 11 ). Upon visible light irradiation, both the MIL-101(Fe) and N,S:CQD supports are photoexcited to generate charge carriers. First, the introduction of N,S:CQD into MIL-101(Fe) effectively improved the absorption of visible light, which can generate more electron-hole pairs. Second, the excellent interfacial contact and high surface area of the composite induced the photogenerated electrons from N,S:CQD transfer to MIL-101(Fe), which can facilitate the transformation of the Fe(III) center to Fe(II) and activate PS to produce SO4•− radicals [49]. More importantly, some generated SO4•− radicals can oxidize OH− to generate •OH. Third, the electrons are transported to the surface of N,S:CQD, so the O2 attached to the material generates •O2 − radicals. The generated free radicals can accelerate the decomposition of BPA, and the holes in the valence band can directly react with BPA at the same time. The mechanism of the degradation of BPA molecules by these active species is shown in the following equation.
N , S : CQD
→ hv
h
+
+
e
-
O2 + e- →•O2 −
MIL - 101
Fe
→ hv
e
-
MIL - 101
Fe
+
h
+
MIL - 101
Fe
e-+MIL-101(Fe) → e-(MIL-101(Fe))
Fe(III) + e-(MIL-101(Fe)) → Fe(II)
Fe(II) + S2O8 2-→SO4•−+SO4 2- +Fe(III)
SO4•−+OH– → •OH + SO4 2-
h+/•O2 −/SO4•−/ •OH + BPA → Degradation products

In conclusion, an N,S:CQD-modified MIL-101(Fe) heterostructure nanocomposite was constructed by a facile hydrothermal method and applied to the photocatalytic degradation of BPA under visible light irradiation. This nanocomposite displayed higher photocatalytic activities than the parent MIL-101(Fe). The degradation efficiency of BPA by 3-N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) was obviously increased to 100% when PS was involved in the reaction process; the pseudofirst-order degradation kinetics constant of BPA was approximately 3.6 times as high as that of MIL-101(Fe). The reasons for the improved degradation efficiency may be the higher separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and promotion of the conversion of Fe(III) to Fe(II) in MIL-101(Fe) of N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe), which facilitated PS to produce free radicals. Compared to SO4•− radicals, h+ and •O2 − play a major role in the process of BPA degradation in the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe)/PS/Vis system. In addition, the N,S:CQD/MIL-101(Fe) composites also manifested good stability and recycling performance with a low level of iron leaching under neutral conditions. This work provides a promising method to design highly efficient MOF-based heterostructural photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.

He Jiang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yan Zhong: Methodology, Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration. Kaixun Tian: Resources, Software, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Haoliang Pang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yaoyao Hao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical review of reductant-enhanced peroxide activation processes: trade-off between accelerated Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle and quenching reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhou, H. Zhang, Y. He, B. Huang, C. Zhou, G. Yao, B. Lai, Critical review of reductant-enhanced peroxide activation processes: Trade-off between accelerated Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle and quenching reactions, Appl. Catal. B, 286 (2021) 119900.
